text:
8.2 Uniqueness check

n_unique_pairs <- nrow(distinct(profiles, year, id))
cat(glue(
  "Unique (year, id) pairs: {n_unique_pairs} of {nrow(profiles)} ",
  "({if (n_unique_pairs == nrow(profiles)) 'PASS — no duplicates' else 'FAIL — duplicates present'})\n"
))

title: Building the 9,000-profile pool by pooling multiple GSS cross-sections
text:
8.3 Marginal distributions vs. weighted 2024 GSS
We use the 2024 wave (most recent) as the reference population. The pooled sample should approximate 2024-weighted GSS marginals on key variables; deviations are expected because older waves represent slightly different demographic compositions.

gss_2024 <- gss_complete |> filter(year == 2024)

validate_marginal <- function(var) {
  src <- gss_2024 |>
    count(.data[[var]], wt = wtssps) |>
    mutate(prop_gss2024 = n / sum(n)) |>
    select(-n)
  prof <- profiles |>
    count(.data[[var]]) |>
    mutate(prop_pool = n / sum(n)) |>
    select(-n)
  full_join(src, prof, by = var) |>
    mutate(diff = round(prop_pool - prop_gss2024, 4),
           prop_gss2024 = round(prop_gss2024, 4),
           prop_pool    = round(prop_pool, 4))
}

vars_to_check <- c("sex", "racecen1", "degree", "partyid", "polviews", "region")

walk(vars_to_check, \(v) {
  cat("\n----", v, "----\n")
  print(validate_marginal(v))
})

title: Clone profiles
text:
The file below contains 9,000 demographically representative U.S. profiles drawn from four General Social Survey waves (2018, 2021, 2022, 2024) using within-year normalized sampling weights. Each profile is assigned to exactly one experimental condition (between-subjects) and encodes the following characteristics: age, sex, race/ethnicity, Hispanic origin, educational attainment, household income, household size, self-identified social class, U.S. region, community type, partisan identification, political ideology, religious affiliation, religious intensity, and scientific consensus acceptance. These profiles serve as inputs for LLM clone simulation; prompt construction is handled separately.
Download profiles.csv

title: Marginal Quotas for Clone Sampling: Census vs. GSS
text:
This script produces non-crossed (marginal) quotas for the six profile variables we need for an alternative prompting strategy: AGE, GENDER, PARTY, RACE, EDUCATION, POLITICAL_ORIENTATION.
We compute each marginal twice:

Census-based quotas, using tidycensus against the most recent Census Population Estimates (vintage 2024) for gender, age, race, and the most recent ACS 1-year release for education. Census has no measure of party or political orientation, so those rows are NA on the Census side.
GSS-based quotas, computed from the most recent GSS wave with post-stratification weights (wtssps). GSS covers all six variables.

The script binds the two sets and saves a comparison table.

title: Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs
text:
Abstract
We test the potential of predicting human behavior in survey experiments with behavioral clones—large language model (LLM) generated artificial agents mimicking human participants. To evaluate this potential, we predict the outcomes of a large-scale field experiment (N = ~22,000) to be conducted in the United States. The experiment will test 20 text-based interventions designed to increase trust in climate scientists, assessing a broad range of secondary, outcomes including self-reported attitudes (e.g., support for funding of climate science) and behavioral measures (e.g., donations to a scientific organization). Before the human study is fielded, we will simulate it with behavioral clones using demographically representative U.S. participant profiles. We will lock the simulated dataset and analysis code. Once human data are available, we will compare clone-based predictions to human results using a set of preregistered metrics spanning (i) inferential agreement and estimate convergence on treatment effects, (ii) outcome distributions, and (iii) subgroup heterogeneity. Our study will provide a large-scale, credible benchmark test of LLMs’ potential—and limitations—as tools for simulating human subjects in social science research. All analyses in this preregistration are demonstrated on simulated placeholder data with identical structure to the real datasets; no human outcome data have been observed at the time of registration.

title: Predicting the effects of a megastudy with behavioral clones
text: We test the potential of predicting human behavior in survey experiments with behavioral clones—large language model (LLM) generated artificial agents mimicking individual respondents. To evaluate this potential, we predict the outcomes of a large-scale field experiment (N = ~21,000) to be conducted in the United States. The experiment will test 20 text-based interventions designed to increase trust in climate scientists, assessing a broad range of outcomes including self-reported attitudes (e.g., trust in scientists) and behavioral measures (e.g., donations to a scientific organization). Before running the experiment with human subjects, we will simulate it with behavioral clones representative of the US population. We will register these simulated data and compare it to the human data, once available. Our evaluation criteria include average treatment effects, response distributions, and subgroup heterogeneity. Our study will provide a large-scale, credible benchmark test of LLMs’ potential—and limitations—as tools for simulating human subjects in social science research. Preliminary results will be available at the time of the Behavioral Clones Workshop (simulations in February, human data collection in March 2026).

title: Achievements for SNSF CV template
text:
ADVANCEMENT OF KNOWLEDGE ON PUBLIC TRUST IN SCIENCE. Given the individual and social cost of a lack of trust in science, research has focused on understanding why some people do not trust science. This focus has left a basic puzzle largely unaddressed: Why do most people tend to trust science, in spite of knowing so little about it? My PhD research has made substantive contributions to answer this question. I have been a collaborator in a manylabs study establishing that, across the globe, public trust in science is moderately high (Cologna et al., 2025). In another study, I have shown that, in the United States, even people who say they do not trust science accept essentially all of basic scientific knowledge, (e.g., the relative size of electrons and atoms Pfänder, Kerzreho, et al., 2025). Through a series of first-author publications (Pfänder, Rouilhan, et al., 2025; Pfänder, De Courson, et al., 2025; Pfänder & Mercier, 2025b, 2025a), I have developed a cognitive model of how people form trust in science and scientific expertise—the rational impression account. According to this account, people do not need a profound understanding or detailed knowledge of science to rationally trust it. Instead, by appealing to basic cognitive mechanisms of information evaluation, science impresses people, who then mostly forget the information that had impressed them. This account stresses the importance of public exposure to science via science education and communication.



FOSTERING OPEN SCIENCE PRACTICES. In my research, I am committed to adhering to the highest standards of open science. All of my publications are developed as fully computationally reproducible projects, with open access to materials, data, and code. In an effort to contribute to cumulative science in the field of misinformation research, I have lead an already widely cited meta-analysis on people’s veracity judgments of online news (Pfänder & Altay, 2025). Beyond transparency, I view open science as inherently collaborative. I actively contribute to collective research efforts that pool data, expertise, and methodological resources to produce more robust, inclusive, and consensual scientific knowledge. In this spirit, I coordinated data collection for the French sample in a manylabs study on trust in science (Cologna et al., 2025) and served as a data analyst on a large collaborative project examining the reproducibility of scientific findings, currently in press at Nature (Aczel et al., 2025).



MENTORING AND TEACHING. Alongside my research activities, I have developed a strong teaching and mentoring profile. For the last three years, I have been the sole instructor of the undergraduate course Fact-Checking and Critical Thinking at Université (Paris)—a course which I created. The class provided hands-on training in research design, data analysis, and statistics. In addition, I have supervised five undergraduate student internships, supporting students in developing research questions, data analysis skills, and academic writing. My only graduate student supervision resulted in an academic publication (Pfänder, Kerzreho, et al., 2025).
